family: Fries-, Photo-Fries, and Anionic Ortho-Fries Rearrangement
rationale: This is the classic Fries rearrangement of phenyl acetate to 2-hydroxyacetophenone, catalyzed by aluminum chloride. It is the simplest and most frequently cited textbook example. The reaction involves the intramolecular rearrangement of an acyl group from a phenolic oxygen to an ortho position on the aromatic ring. This example clearly demonstrates the core transformation of a phenolic ester to a hydroxy aryl ketone, which is the defining feature of the Fries rearrangement.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses phenyl acetate, the simplest aromatic ester substrate, to avoid overlap with more complex aromatic substitution reactions. The product is a simple hydroxyacetophenone, avoiding motifs like polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons or complex functional groups that could trigger other named reaction queries. The use of a simple acyl group (acetyl) prevents confusion with reactions involving more complex migrating groups.
